stimulus:
Text 1

Herman Melville's 1857 novel The Confidence-Man stands apart from his other major works. His earlier novels build tightly around extended seafaring adventures and probe their characters' inner lives at length, whereas The Confidence-Man proceeds through a loosely linked series of conversations on a Mississippi steamboat and renders its characters' minds far more obliquely. Melville himself sometimes treated the novel as a minor experiment, once describing it dismissively as "a curiosity."

Text 2

Like Melville's other great novels, The Confidence-Man dramatizes how people negotiate trust in a world full of strangers. Like those earlier works, it scrutinizes the deceptions people enact for themselves and for one another. Though its tone is sharper, more sardonic—the comedy of a sustained con rather than the grandeur of a hunted whale—this seeming "curiosity" engages no less seriously with the moral preoccupations that animate the handful of Melville's novels now regarded as classics.
stem: Based on the texts, how would the author of Text 2 most likely respond to the assessment of The Confidence-Man presented in Text 1?

answerOptions.content: By agreeing that The Confidence-Man is less impressive than certain other novels by Melville but arguing that it should still be regarded as a classic
By concurring that the reputation of The Confidence-Man as a minor work has led readers to overlook this novel but maintaining that the reputation is unearned
By acknowledging that The Confidence-Man clearly differs from Melville's other major novels but insisting on its centrality to his body of work nonetheless
By conceding that Melville's talents were best suited to seafaring novels but asserting that the comic register of The Confidence-Man is often effective

rationale:
Choice C is the best answer. Both texts agree that The Confidence-Man is unusual for Melville: Text 1 calls it an outlier and a minor experiment, while Text 2 describes its tone as sharper and more sardonic. But Text 2 also insists the novel engages with the moral preoccupations that animate Melville's recognized classics, so its author would likely accept that The Confidence-Man differs from Melville's other novels while insisting on its importance.

Choice A is incorrect because Text 2 does not portray The Confidence-Man as less impressive; it claims the novel engages just as seriously with Melville's themes. Choice B is incorrect because neither text suggests that readers have generally overlooked the novel. Choice D is incorrect because Text 2 does not concede that Melville's talents were best suited to seafaring novels; it compares The Confidence-Man favorably to those works.
